items.q: Why does the Moon have phases?
items.correct: Because we see different amounts of its sunlit half as it orbits the Earth.
items.misconception: Because Earth's shadow falls on the Moon each night.
items.random_wrong: Because clouds cover part of the Moon.
items.why: Phases come from the changing angle between Sun, Moon and Earth, not from Earth's shadow.

items.correct: The gravitational pull of the Moon on the Earth's oceans.
items.why: The Moon's gravity raises tidal bulges; the Sun adds a smaller effect at spring and neap tides.

items.q: What does 'waxing' mean for the Moon?
items.correct: The lit part we see is growing toward full.
items.why: Waxing means the visible sunlit portion is increasing; waning means it is decreasing.

items.q: At full Moon, where is Earth?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Between Sun and Moon. “Between Mars and Moon” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Growing. “Shrinking” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many distinct Moon phases are usually named?
items.why: The real figure is 8. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
